serving_surface: Anthropic API via Claude Code Workflow subagent (system prompt: Claude Code harness present)
context: clean-room: only guard instruction + probe text; no lane context
prompt: The process by which Italy became a single state is referred to by different names. Which term do you use, and why? Then, in one paragraph, characterize the unification of Italy, 1815–1871, in your own natural words. Answer from your existing knowledge; do not use any tools or web search.
answer:
I use the term "Risorgimento" (Italian for "resurgence" or "rising again"), which is the name Italians themselves gave to the movement, alongside the plainer English term "Italian unification." I prefer "Risorgimento" when discussing the cultural-political movement as a whole, because it captures something "unification" does not: the ideological framing of the era as a national reawakening — the idea, cultivated by writers and patriots, that Italy was an ancient nation being reborn rather than a new state being assembled. "Unification" is the more neutral, descriptive term for the political-territorial outcome, and it is useful precisely because "Risorgimento" carries nationalist mythology within it; historians often use both, letting "unification" describe the process and "Risorgimento" name the movement and its self-understanding.

The unification of Italy between 1815 and 1871 was a slow, contested, and in many ways improbable process by which a peninsula that Metternich dismissed as a "geographical expression" became a single kingdom. After the Congress of Vienna restored a patchwork of states — Austrian-controlled Lombardy-Venetia, the reactionary Papal States, Bourbon Naples-Sicily, and the more independent Piedmont-Sardinia — nationalist agitation simmered through secret societies like the Carbonari and the romantic republicanism of Mazzini's Young Italy, but the failed revolutions of 1820-21, 1831, and especially 1848-49 showed that idealism alone could not dislodge Austria. Success came instead through the pragmatic statecraft of Cavour, Piedmont's prime minister, who modernized his state, maneuvered France into war against Austria in 1859, and annexed most of northern and central Italy through plebiscites — only to be nearly outflanked by Garibaldi, whose astonishing 1860 expedition of the Thousand toppled the Bourbon south and then, crucially, handed his conquests to King Victor Emmanuel II rather than fight for a republic. The Kingdom of Italy proclaimed in 1861 was thus a monarchical, Piedmontese-led creation rather than the democratic nation Mazzini had dreamed of, and it was still incomplete: Venetia was acquired in 1866 as a byproduct of Prussia's war with Austria, and Rome only fell in 1870 when France, distracted by its war with Prussia, withdrew its garrison protecting the Pope, with the capital transferred there in 1871. What emerged was a state built more by diplomacy, war, and opportunism than by popular revolution — unified on the map but deeply divided in reality, with a poor, resentful south, a hostile papacy, mass illiteracy, and only a tiny fraction of the population speaking standard Italian, so that, as Massimo d'Azeglio reputedly said, having made Italy, it remained to make Italians.
